1: The oracle is temporally precise: Nebuchadnezzar and his entire coalition (kol-mamlachot eretz memshelet yado, all the kingdoms and peoples under his dominion) — a comprehensive reference to the multi-national Babylonian army described in 2 Kgs 24:2-3 and historically confirmed by the Babylonian Chronicles. The siege of Jerusalem is the backdrop against which Zedekiah receives both a word of judgment and a qualified mercy.
2: hineni noten et-ha'ir hazot beyad melech Bavel visrafah ba-esh: 'I am going to hand this city over to the king of Babylon, and he will burn it with fire.' The hand-over formula (noten beyad) is the standard divine speech act for delivering a city to an enemy — the same formula used for Israel's victories in the wilderness (Josh 6:2: 'I have given Jericho into your hand'). Here it is inverted: YHWH delivers his own city. The burning of the city (sarafah ba-esh) is the covenant curse of Deut 13:16 applied to an apostate city — Jerusalem is treated as an ir hanidachat, a city devoted to destruction.
3: velo timalet miyado ki tafas titapes uveyadoh titten: 'You will not escape his grasp but will certainly be captured and handed over to him.' The double infinitive absolute (tafas titapes, captured you will be captured) intensifies the certainty. The personal encounter — 'your eyes will look into the eyes of the king of Babylon, and he will speak with you in person' (peh el peh yedaber imach) — is the intimate horror of defeat: face-to-face with your conqueror. The prophecy was fulfilled precisely: Zedekiah was captured, brought before Nebuchadnezzar, and had his sons killed before his eyes before being blinded (39:5-7; 52:9-11; 2 Kgs 25:6-7).
4: ach shema dvar YHWH Tzidqiyahu melech Yehudah: 'Yet hear the word of the LORD, O Zedekiah king of Judah.' The adversative ach (yet/however) pivots from judgment to the qualified mercy: even for a king whose dynasty is ending, YHWH has a specific word. The content following is not rescue but a mercy within the judgment — a mitigated death rather than a brutal execution.
5: beshalom tamut uvemisrefot avoteicha hamelachim harishonim: 'You will die in peace. And as spices were burned in honor of your ancestors, the kings who preceded you, so they will burn spices for you and mourn for you, crying, 'Alas, lord!'' Beshalom tamut (you will die in peace) means death by natural causes rather than by sword — a qualified mercy within judgment. The burning of spices for a king's funeral was a royal honor (2 Chron 16:14 for Asa; 21:19 for Jehoram's failure to receive this honor). Zedekiah receives the minimum of royal dignity in death. The oracle was literally fulfilled: Zedekiah was blinded and imprisoned in Babylon until his death (39:7; 52:11), dying there rather than by execution.
6: The note of Jeremiah's public proclamation of this oracle to Zedekiah — while Babylonian armies besieged Jerusalem — underscores the prophetic courage required. The oracle contains both bad news (capture, exile) and qualified good news (survival), making it a message no political or military authority would want proclaimed publicly.
7: beihilachem chel melech Bavel al-Yerushalaim veal-kol-arei Yehudah hanissarot al-Lachish ve'al-Azekah: The historical precision: only Lachish and Azekah remained as unconquered fortified cities at this point in the siege. The Lachish Letters (clay ostraca found at Lachish, dated to this period) contain a poignant note from a military commander: 'We are watching for the signals of Lachish according to all the indications my lord has given, because we cannot see the signals of Azekah.' Azekah had already fallen when that letter was written — providing remarkable extra-biblical confirmation of Jeremiah's historical accuracy.
8: hadavar asher hayah el-Yirmiyahu me'et YHWH achar karat hamelech Tzidqiyahu berit et-kol-ha'am asher biYerushalaim liqro lahem deror: 'The word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD after King Zedekiah had made a covenant with all the people in Jerusalem to proclaim liberty.' Deror (liberty/release) is the technical term of the Jubilee year (Lev 25:10: 'proclaim liberty throughout the land'). The covenant to release Hebrew slaves invokes this Jubilee framework — a remarkable moment of covenant renewal under siege pressure that was immediately reversed.
9: leshallach ish et-avdo ha-ivri ve'et-amatoh ha-ivriyah chofshi: 'Everyone was to release his Hebrew male slave and his Hebrew female slave.' The law behind this action: Exod 21:2 (Hebrew slaves serve six years then go free) and Deut 15:12-18 (the seventh-year shemittah release). The law had apparently been ignored for an extended period — the act of liberation under siege pressure suggests accumulated unpaid obligations, perhaps spanning many sabbatical years. The specificity of 'Hebrew' (fellow-covenant-member) slavery makes the violation doubly problematic: enslaving one's own covenant kin.
10: The brief moment of obedience: the officials and people entered the covenant (kol-hasarim vekhal-ha-am asher ba'u vaberit) and released their slaves. The sequence — covenant-making, liberation, release — is the pattern of covenant renewal (cf. Neh 10 where similar obligations are undertaken). The response was real and public.
11: vayashuvu acharei-chen vayashivu et-ha'avadim ve'et-ha'amahos asher shilechu chofshim vayichabshum la'avadim vela'amahos: 'But afterward they turned back and took back the male and female slaves they had freed, forcing them back into slavery.' The reversal is almost immediately: the Babylonian army temporarily lifted the siege (probably to deal with an Egyptian relief force, cf. 37:5-11), and the slave-owners interpreted the lifting of pressure as permission to rescind their covenant vows. The economic calculation overrode the covenant obligation. The irony is profound: they freed the slaves when the enemy was at the gates; they re-enslaved them when the enemy temporarily withdrew.
12: The messenger formula signals YHWH's direct response to the breach — the divine word comes specifically because of what happened in vv. 10-11. The response takes the form of ironically mirroring the slave-release with a 'release' of judgment against the slave-owners (v. 17).
13: koh amar YHWH elohe Yisrael anochi karati berit et-avoteichem beyom hotzi oti otam me-eretz Mitzraim mibet avadim: 'Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel: I made a covenant with your ancestors when I brought them out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.' The appeal to the Exodus covenant grounds the slave-release law in the foundational liberation narrative. Israel's entire existence as a people begins with being freed from slavery by YHWH — this is why they cannot enslave each other indefinitely. Beit avadim (house of slavery) is the standard Exodus identification phrase (Exod 13:3, 14; 20:2; Deut 5:6). Those who were freed must not make their fellows the slaves YHWH freed them from being.
14: miqetz sheva shanim teshalechu ish et-achiv ha-ivri asher yimacher lecha: 'At the end of seven years, every one of you must release a fellow Hebrew who has been sold to you.' The Sabbatical-year law (Deut 15:12-18; Exod 21:2): six years of service, release in the seventh. The social logic: the number seven embeds the release in the Sabbath structure — just as the land rests every seventh year (Lev 25), so the enslaved person is released. The violation is not merely economic but cosmological: it disrupts the Sabbatical rhythm built into creation. The indictment 'your fathers did not listen to me or give me their attention' (velo shemu avoteichem elai velo hitu et-aznam) establishes a centuries-long pattern of ignoring this law.
15: vatashvu atem hayom vata'asu et-hayashar be-einai liqro deror ish le-re'ehu: 'And you — just recently you turned back and did what was right in my eyes, each of you proclaiming liberty to your neighbor.' The acknowledgment of the momentary act of covenant obedience: 'you did what was right in my eyes' (ha-yashar be-einai) — the highest covenant commendation (used of David in 1 Kgs 14:8; 15:5). The covenant was made in the house bearing YHWH's name (la-shem alav), the temple — the public, sacred context that maximizes the gravity of the subsequent breach.
16: vatashuvah vatechalelu et-shemi vatashivu ish et-avdo ve'ish et-amatoh: 'But then you turned back and profaned my name: each of you took back the male and female slaves you had set free.' Chalal shem YHWH (profane/desecrate the name of the LORD) — the same term for violating what is holy (Lev 18:21; 19:12; 20:3; Ezek 36:20-23). Making a covenant oath before YHWH and then breaking it is not merely economic recidivism but blasphemy: it treats YHWH's name as surety for a promise and then repudiates both the promise and the surety.
17: lachen koh-amar YHWH atem lo-shema'tem elai liqro deror ish el-achiv ve-ish el-re'ehu hineni qore lachem deror neum YHWH el-hacherev el-hadever ve'el-hara'av: 'Therefore thus says the LORD: You have not obeyed me by proclaiming liberty ... So now I am proclaiming liberty for you — liberty to the sword, to pestilence, and to famine.' The bitter irony: the Hebrew word deror (liberty) used of the slave-release is now applied to the sword, famine, and pestilence — they are 'liberated' to range freely among the people. The covenant logic of Lev 26:25 is enacted: the covenant curses 'avenge the covenant' when it is broken. Refusing to proclaim deror to people results in YHWH proclaiming deror to the judgments against them.
18: venatati et-ha-anashim haovrim et-beriti asher lo-qamu et-divrei haberit asher karu lifnai levein batrei ha-egel asher karu lishtayim: 'And I will hand over the men who violated my covenant ... when they cut the calf in two and passed between its parts.' The covenant-cutting ceremony (karat berit, literally 'to cut a covenant') with its divided animal is the Gen 15 pattern: YHWH himself passes between the animal halves in the form of the smoking fire-pot and flaming torch, making himself the implied sacrifice if the covenant is violated. Here the humans passed between the parts — making themselves the implied sacrifice. YHWH is calling in the covenant sanction: those who passed between the calf-halves will become like the calf — cut in pieces, exposed as carrion (v. 20).
19: The comprehensive list of those who passed between the calf halves — officials of Judah, officials of Jerusalem, court officials, priests, and all the people — establishes that the covenant-oath violation is community-wide, not confined to a few individuals. The entire leadership structure of Judah participated in the broken oath, making the judgment comprehensive.
20: venatati otam beyad oyveihem uveyad mevaqashei nafisham vehayetah nivlatam lema-achal le-of hashamayim ulivehmat ha-aretz: 'I will give them into the hands of their enemies ... Their dead bodies will become food for the birds of the sky and the beasts of the earth.' The death without burial is the covenant curse of Deut 28:26: 'your carcasses will be food for all the birds of the air and the beasts of the earth.' It also mirrors the fate the Babylonian armies threatened Jerusalem with, turned back on those who made covenant-oaths and broke them. The birds and beasts consume what was supposed to be buried — the final degradation of covenant violation.
21: ve-et Tzidqiyahu melech Yehudah ve-et-sarav ettein beyad oyveihem: The king and his officials receive the same fate as the slave-owners — inclusion in the judgment is comprehensive across social strata. The army that 'pulled back from you' (ha-olim me-aleichem) is the temporary Babylonian withdrawal to deal with the Egyptian relief force — the very event that prompted the slave-owners to rescind their liberation. The army's return is announced in v. 22.
22: hineni metzaveh neum YHWH vahashivotim el-ha-ir hazot venilchamu aleiha: 'I am about to give the command, declares the LORD, and I will bring them back to this city. They will fight against it, take it, and burn it with fire.' The divine command that turns the Babylonian army around: YHWH directs the movements of foreign armies as instruments of covenant judgment (cf. Isa 10:5-6: 'Woe to Assyria, the rod of my anger'; Isa 45:1: Cyrus as YHWH's anointed). The certainty of the return is grounded not in military intelligence but in divine decree.
